notes: Tentatively associated with seasonal affective disorder. Evidence is limited — SAD genetics are poorly understood and most studies have been underpowered. The condition is strongly influenced by latitude, light exposure, and serotonin pathway function. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.phenotype: Standard MAPT Haplotype — Reference Tau Regulation
AA.description: Homozygous reference; standard MAPT haplotype context on chromosome 17q21. Normal tau protein expression, splicing, and microtubule binding. Baseline population risk for tauopathy-associated neurodegenerative conditions.

AG.description: Heterozygous AG; one G allele at this MAPT/SPPL2C region position. Intermediate haplotype context between the H1 and H2 MAPT haplotype blocks, with corresponding intermediate tau expression and splicing patterns.

GG.phenotype: Alternate MAPT Haplotype — Altered Tau Regulation
GG.description: Homozygous G allele; alternative MAPT region haplotype associated with altered tau protein isoform balance and expression levels. The MAPT locus H1 haplotype is associated with increased risk for progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), corticobasal degeneration (CBD), and Parkinson's disease.

description: rs12185268 maps to chromosome 17q21 near MAPT (microtubule-associated protein tau) and SPPL2C. MAPT encodes tau, the microtubule-stabilizing protein that forms neurofibrillary tangles in Alzheimer's disease and other tauopathies. The chromosome 17q21 region contains two major MAPT haplotype blocks (H1 and H2) associated with differential risk for progressive supranuclear palsy, corticobasal degeneration, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease. Common variants in this region serve as proxies for MAPT haplotype membership and associated tau expression and splicing differences.

sources.name: Baker M et al. (1999) - Association of an extended haplotype in the tau gene with progressive supranuclear palsy

notes: dbSNP maps rs12185268 to chromosome 17 near SPPL2C/MAPT-AS1, adjacent to the MAPT locus. Prior annotation linking this rsID to ATXN2 or chromosome 12 was based on an incorrect mapping and has been corrected. The MAPT H1 haplotype is one of the best-established genetic risk factors for tauopathies including PSP and CBD (OR ~5 for PSP). ALS associations at this locus are not established — the ATXN2/ALS connection involves chromosome 12 and is a separate gene/locus. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: Increased alpha-synuclein levels, elevated Parkinson's disease and Lewy body risk

AG.description: Moderate alpha-synuclein levels, slightly elevated PD risk

GG.description: Normal alpha-synuclein expression, population-average Parkinson's risk

description: SNCA 3' UTR variant modulates alpha-synuclein mRNA stability and expression. Gene dosage is critical — duplications and triplications cause familial Parkinson's with increasing severity.
notes: Alpha-synuclein aggregation into Lewy bodies is the hallmark of PD pathology. Even modest expression increases (~1.5x) from common variants contribute to population PD risk. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: H1/H1 MAPT haplotype, slightly increased risk of progressive supranuclear palsy and tauopathies

AG.description: Mixed MAPT haplotype, intermediate tauopathy risk

GG.description: H2/H2 MAPT haplotype, reduced risk of tauopathies and Parkinson's disease

description: MAPT H1/H2 haplotype tag SNP affects tau protein expression and splicing. H1 is associated with increased risk of tauopathies including progressive supranuclear palsy and Parkinson's disease.
notes: The H1 haplotype spans a 1.8Mb inversion on chromosome 17. H2 frequency ~20% in Europeans but very rare in East Asian and African populations. H1 increases tau expression and 4-repeat tau isoforms. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Reduced hippocampal efficiency during memory encoding, lower working memory scores

CT.description: Intermediate hippocampal activation and memory performance

TT.description: Enhanced hippocampal memory consolidation, better working memory and learning rate

description: KIBRA (WWC1) variant affects working memory and learning rate through modulation of synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus. Replicated memory-associated gene.
notes: KIBRA T allele carriers show better episodic memory performance across multiple studies. The protein interacts with PKMzeta and dendrin at postsynaptic densities, modulating long-term potentiation. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

GG.description: Normal ALDH2 enzyme function, no flush reaction

GA.description: Reduced ALDH2 activity, facial flushing with alcohol, increased hangover, higher cancer risk

AA.description: Very low ALDH2 activity, severe facial flushing, nausea with small amounts of alcohol, strongly protective against alcoholism

description: ALDH2*2 variant (Glu504Lys) causing aldehyde dehydrogenase deficiency. Common in East Asians (~40% carriers). Leads to acetaldehyde accumulation and 'Asian flush' reaction.

sources.name: Yoshida A et al. (1984) - Molecular abnormality of an inactive aldehyde dehydrogenase variant commonly found in Orientals

notes: ALDH2*2 variant causing the 'Asian flush' reaction to alcohol. Nearly 40% of East Asians carry at least one copy. Homozygotes have severe alcohol intolerance. Also associated with increased esophageal cancer risk with alcohol consumption.

CC.description: Normal melanocortin-4 receptor function and appetite regulation.

CT.description: One copy of V103I gain-of-function variant; associated with ~18% reduced obesity risk.

TT.description: Two copies of V103I protective variant; associated with further reduced obesity risk.

description: The rs2229616 variant (Val103Ile) in the MC4R gene is a gain-of-function polymorphism that enhances melanocortin receptor signaling. Carriers are protected against obesity, with meta-analyses showing an approximately 18% reduced risk per copy of the Ile103 allele.

sources.name: Heid IM et al. (2005) - Association of the MC4R V103I polymorphism with the metabolic syndrome

notes: This is a protective variant, not a risk factor. The T allele (Ile103) is rare (~1.9% globally) but consistent in its protective effect across multiple large meta-analyses. Unlike the loss-of-function MC4R mutations that cause severe monogenic obesity, this gain-of-function variant enhances hypothalamic satiety signaling. Discuss with a healthcare provider for personalized interpretation.

CC.description: Normal BCO1 cleavage activity, efficient conversion of beta-carotene to retinal

CT.description: ~30% reduced BCO1 activity, less efficient beta-carotene to vitamin A conversion

TT.description: ~50% reduced BCO1 activity, poor beta-carotene conversion, may need preformed vitamin A sources

description: BCMO1 (BCO1) A379V variant reduces beta-carotene oxygenase activity. Combined with rs12934922, determines overall efficiency of plant-derived vitamin A production.
notes: Combined with rs12934922, up to 69% reduction in beta-carotene conversion in double homozygotes. Important for vegans and populations relying on plant-based vitamin A. ~25% carry at least one reduced-function allele.

AA.description: Rapid caffeine breakdown, can drink coffee late without sleep issues, may need more for effects

CC.description: Slow caffeine breakdown, caffeine effects last longer, may have sleep issues with afternoon coffee, increased hypertension risk with high intake

description: CYP1A2*1F variant determining caffeine metabolism speed. AA (fast) metabolizers clear caffeine ~4x faster than CC (slow). Affects coffee tolerance and cardiovascular effects.

sources.name: Sachse C et al. (1999) - Functional significance of a C→A polymorphism in intron 1 of the cytochrome P450 CYP1A2 gene

notes: CYP1A2 variant determining fast vs slow caffeine metabolism. Fast metabolizers (AA genotype) clear caffeine ~4x faster. Clinically relevant — slow metabolizers may have increased cardiovascular risk with high coffee intake. Affects drug metabolism beyond caffeine.

TT.description: Lower habitual coffee intake

CC.description: Higher habitual coffee consumption tendency

description: Aryl hydrocarbon receptor variant influencing caffeine reward pathway and consumption habits.

sources.name: Cornelis MC et al. (2011) - Genome-wide meta-analysis identifies regions on 7p21 (AHR) and 15q24 (CYP1A2) as determinants of habitual caffeine consumption

notes: AHR variant associated with habitual coffee consumption in large GWAS. AHR regulates CYP1A2 expression, linking this to caffeine metabolism. Effect is consistent across populations. Coffee consumption is also heavily influenced by cultural and socioeconomic factors.

TT.description: Normal alcohol metabolism

TC.description: Faster alcohol to acetaldehyde conversion - mild flush

CC.description: Rapid conversion - strong facial flushing

description: Alcohol dehydrogenase fast-metabolizer variant causing acetaldehyde accumulation.

sources.name: Li H et al. (2009) - Refined geographic distribution of the oriental ALDH2*504Lys (nee 487Lys) variant

notes: ADH1B variant causing rapid alcohol metabolism (Arg48His). Common in East Asian populations (~70-90% frequency). Carriers experience acetaldehyde accumulation causing facial flushing, nausea, and headache with alcohol. Protective against alcoholism but associated with esophageal cancer risk in drinkers.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard GCKR inhibitory activity on glucokinase. Associated with typical fasting glucose levels and lower triglycerides. T allele frequency ~39% globally.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate GCKR regulatory activity. Fasting glucose and triglyceride levels fall between the TT and CC extremes, with modest elevation in hepatic glucose uptake.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; the C allele reduces GCKR inhibitory binding to glucokinase, increasing hepatic glucose uptake and glycolysis. Results in lower fasting glucose but elevated fasting triglycerides due to increased de novo lipogenesis. C allele is more common (~61% globally).

description: GCKR (glucokinase regulatory protein) encodes an inhibitory protein that sequesters hepatic glucokinase in the nucleus, preventing glucose phosphorylation when glucose is low. The rs780094 variant (in strong LD with the functional rs1260326 variant) modulates this inhibitory activity: the C allele reduces GCKR binding to glucokinase, allowing more active hepatic glucose uptake and glycolysis. This produces a paradoxical phenotype — lower fasting glucose but higher fasting triglycerides — due to redirected carbon flux toward de novo lipogenesis.

sources.name: Sparso T et al. (2008) - The GCKR rs780094 polymorphism is associated with elevated fasting serum triacylglycerol, reduced fasting and OGTT-related insulinaemia, and reduced risk of type 2 diabetes

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The CC genotype confers a paradoxical metabolic effect: marginally lower type 2 diabetes risk (due to lower fasting glucose) but elevated triglycerides (mild cardiovascular risk factor). African populations show lowest T allele frequency (~18%), East Asians the highest (~52%). Individuals with CC genotype and family history of hypertriglyceridemia or pancreatitis should discuss lipid monitoring with a healthcare provider. Dietary fat modification can partially offset triglyceride elevation.

CC.description: Higher FADS2 delta-6-desaturase activity, efficient omega-3 and omega-6 processing

CT.description: Moderate FADS2 activity, intermediate fatty acid processing

TT.description: Lower FADS2 activity, less efficient fatty acid conversion, may benefit from direct EPA/DHA intake

description: FADS2 delta-6-desaturase variant affects the rate-limiting step in long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid synthesis from dietary precursors (ALA to EPA, LA to GLA).
notes: FADS2 performs the first desaturation step in both omega-3 and omega-6 pathways. The FADS1/FADS2 gene cluster shows strong signals of positive selection in populations with plant-based diets.

CC.description: Full MTHFR enzyme activity, normal folate and homocysteine metabolism

CT.description: ~65% MTHFR enzyme activity, mildly elevated homocysteine levels

TT.description: ~30% MTHFR enzyme activity, elevated homocysteine, increased folate supplementation needs

description: MTHFR C677T (Ala222Val) reduces methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase activity, impairing folate metabolism and raising homocysteine levels. One of the most clinically relevant nutrigenomic variants.
notes: TT genotype has ~30% residual enzyme activity. Elevated homocysteine is a cardiovascular risk factor. Adequate folate intake can compensate. Important during pregnancy for neural tube defect prevention.

AA.description: Full enzyme activity at A1298C position, normal folate metabolism

AC.description: Slightly reduced MTHFR activity (~85%), mild effect on folate metabolism

CC.description: Reduced MTHFR activity (~60-70%), may compound with C677T for elevated homocysteine

description: MTHFR A1298C (Glu429Ala) variant mildly reduces enzyme activity. Clinically significant mainly when compound heterozygous with C677T, impairing folate-mediated one-carbon metabolism.

sources.name: van der Put NM et al. (1998)

notes: A1298C alone has mild effect. Compound heterozygosity (677CT + 1298AC) can reduce MTHFR activity to ~50%, similar to 677TT alone. The C allele frequency is ~30% in most populations.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; GCKR Pro446 isoform with normal inhibitory binding to glucokinase. Standard fasting glucose regulation and lower de novo lipogenesis. T allele ~40% globally.

TC.description: Heterozygous; one copy of the Leu446 variant partially reduces GCKR's inhibitory binding to glucokinase. Intermediate effect on hepatic glucose metabolism and triglyceride synthesis.

CC.description: Homozygous Leu446; the C allele encodes a GCKR protein (Pro446Leu) with markedly reduced inhibitory binding to glucokinase. Hepatic glucokinase is more constitutively active, driving lower fasting glucose but elevated triglycerides. C allele ~60% globally.

description: rs1260326 encodes the functional GCKR Pro446Leu amino acid variant, the primary causal SNP at this locus. The Leu446 (C allele) isoform of glucokinase regulatory protein has substantially reduced affinity for glucokinase, resulting in persistent hepatic glucokinase activity even during fasting. This increases hepatic glucose phosphorylation, glycolysis, and downstream de novo lipogenesis — lowering fasting plasma glucose while elevating fasting triglycerides. This variant is one of the most replicated metabolic GWAS hits, with consistent effects across diverse populations.

sources.name: Sparso T et al. (2008) - The GCKR rs780094 polymorphism is associated with elevated fasting serum triacylglycerol, reduced fasting and OGTT-related insulinaemia, and reduced risk of type 2 diabetes

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. rs1260326 is the functional causal variant at the GCKR locus (Pro446Leu), while rs780094 is a proxy marker in high LD. The C allele prevalence is ~60% globally (~58% European, ~85% African, ~47% East Asian). The elevated triglyceride association is most pronounced in the context of high carbohydrate or high-fat diets. Individuals with CC genotype and elevated fasting triglycerides should discuss dietary optimization and monitoring with a healthcare provider.

AA.description: Homozygous HFE C282Y, high risk of iron overload and hemochromatosis requiring monitoring/treatment

AG.description: HFE C282Y carrier, mildly elevated iron stores but typically asymptomatic

GG.description: Wild-type HFE, normal iron absorption and regulation

description: HFE C282Y is the primary cause of hereditary hemochromatosis type 1, causing excessive iron absorption that can damage the liver, heart, and pancreas if untreated.
notes: C282Y homozygosity is present in ~0.5% of Northern Europeans. Penetrance is incomplete (~28% of male and ~1% of female homozygotes develop clinical hemochromatosis). Regular phlebotomy prevents complications.

CC.description: Normal PPARgamma activity, standard adipogenesis and insulin sensitivity

CG.description: Mildly reduced PPARgamma activity, improved insulin sensitivity, lower type 2 diabetes risk

GG.description: Reduced PPARgamma activity, better insulin sensitivity, significantly lower type 2 diabetes risk

description: PPARG Pro12Ala variant modulates the master regulator of adipocyte differentiation. The Ala allele slightly reduces receptor activity, paradoxically improving insulin sensitivity and reducing diabetes risk.
notes: Pro12Ala was one of the first validated T2D susceptibility variants. Ala allele frequency ~12% in Europeans. PPARgamma is the target of thiazolidinedione drugs (pioglitazone). Ala reduces T2D risk by ~20%.

AA.description: Higher hepcidin levels from TMPRSS6 variant, reduced intestinal iron absorption, lower iron stores

AG.description: Moderate TMPRSS6 function, intermediate hepcidin and iron regulation

GG.description: Full TMPRSS6/matriptase-2 function, normal hepcidin suppression and iron absorption

description: TMPRSS6 A736V variant affects matriptase-2, a protease that cleaves hemojuvelin to suppress hepcidin (the master iron regulatory hormone). Reduced TMPRSS6 means higher hepcidin and lower iron absorption.
notes: TMPRSS6 is the strongest GWAS hit for serum iron, hemoglobin, and transferrin saturation. The A allele raises hepcidin, reducing iron absorption. Relevant for iron deficiency anemia susceptibility.

CC.description: Lactase production stops after childhood, high likelihood of lactose intolerance (>90%)

CT.description: Lactase persistence, can digest lactose in adulthood

TT.description: Strong lactase persistence, very likely can digest lactose throughout life

description: MCM6 regulatory variant controlling LCT (lactase) gene expression. The -13910 C>T variant enables lactase persistence into adulthood in ~35% of world population, mostly Europeans and some African/Middle Eastern groups.

sources.name: Enattah NS et al. (2002) - Identification of a variant associated with adult-type hypolactasia

notes: The primary lactase persistence variant in European populations (-13910C>T). T allele allows continued milk digestion into adulthood. Different lactase persistence mutations exist in African and Middle Eastern populations. One of the best examples of recent human adaptation.

CC.description: Higher FADS1 activity, efficient conversion of plant-based omega-3 (ALA) to EPA/DHA

CT.description: Moderate FADS1 activity, intermediate omega-3/omega-6 conversion efficiency

TT.description: Lower FADS1 activity, less efficient omega-3 conversion, may benefit more from direct fish oil supplementation

description: FADS1 fatty acid desaturase 1 variant affects the conversion efficiency of plant-based omega-3 (ALA) to EPA and DHA, and omega-6 (LA) to arachidonic acid.
notes: FADS1/FADS2 cluster variants show strong signals of recent natural selection. The efficient-desaturation allele was selected for in populations with plant-based diets. Affects blood lipid profiles.

CC.description: Normal BCMO1 activity, efficient conversion of beta-carotene to retinal (vitamin A)

CT.description: ~30% reduced BCMO1 activity, less efficient beta-carotene to vitamin A conversion

TT.description: ~50% reduced BCMO1 activity, significantly less efficient provitamin A conversion, may need preformed vitamin A

description: BCMO1 (BCO1) variant reduces beta-carotene 15,15'-oxygenase activity, the enzyme that cleaves beta-carotene into retinal. Poor converters may not get adequate vitamin A from plant sources alone.
notes: ~45% of the population carries at least one reduced-function allele. Combined with rs7501331, up to 69% reduction in conversion. Important for vegans relying on plant-based vitamin A (beta-carotene).

CG.description: Intermediate effect, moderately elevated postprandial triglyceride response to dietary fat

CC.description: Standard triglyceride metabolism, normal postprandial lipemia

GG.description: Significantly higher triglyceride response to fat intake, increased cardiovascular risk

description: ZPR1/APOA5 variant affects triglyceride metabolism and postprandial lipemia. APOA5 regulates triglyceride-rich lipoprotein clearance by activating lipoprotein lipase.
notes: This locus has one of the largest effects on triglyceride levels from GWAS. Risk allele carriers show ~20% higher fasting triglycerides. APOA5 activates LPL-mediated triglyceride clearance from VLDL and chylomicrons.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; baseline pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) plasma levels associated with standard ALPL tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase activity. Most common in African populations (~87% C frequency).

CT.description: Heterozygous; one T allele associated with moderately elevated plasma PLP levels due to partially reduced alkaline phosphatase activity that normally degrades circulating PLP.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with the highest plasma pyridoxal-5'-phosphate levels. The T allele reduces alkaline phosphatase-mediated PLP catabolism. T allele frequency is ~45-53% globally, with highest frequency in East Asians (~53%).

description: The NBPF3/ALPL locus on chromosome 1 harbors rs4654748, a variant associated with circulating plasma pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) levels — the active form of vitamin B6. ALPL (tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase) degrades circulating PLP; reduced ALPL activity from the T allele leads to higher plasma B6 levels. Vitamin B6 is essential for over 100 enzymatic reactions including amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and one-carbon metabolism. Genome-wide association studies consistently identify this locus as the top hit for circulating B6 levels.

sources.name: Tanaka T et al. (2013) - Genome-wide association study of plasma B6 levels identifies novel loci in the NBPF3/ALPL region

notes: Strong population stratification: T allele ~49% in Europeans, ~13% in Africans, ~53% in East Asians. Elevated plasma B6 from TT genotype is generally not harmful and may be beneficial for those with marginally low dietary intake. However, very high-dose B6 supplementation (>100 mg/day) can cause peripheral neuropathy regardless of genotype. The clinically actionable insight is that TT individuals may maintain adequate B6 status with lower dietary intake. Discuss supplementation strategies with a healthcare provider.

GG.description: Higher vitamin D binding protein, more total 25(OH)D but similar bioavailable vitamin D

GT.description: Moderate vitamin D binding protein levels

TT.description: Lower vitamin D binding protein, lower total 25(OH)D but similar bioavailable vitamin D

description: GC (group-specific component) encodes vitamin D binding protein, the primary carrier of 25(OH)D in blood. Variants affect total vitamin D measurements but may not change bioavailable vitamin D.
notes: GC is the strongest genetic determinant of total serum 25(OH)D. However, if free/bioavailable vitamin D is the active form, GC genotype may be less clinically relevant. The Gc2 allele is more common in darker-skinned populations.

AA.description: Higher vitamin D levels

CC.description: Lower vitamin D levels - may need supplementation

description: Vitamin D binding protein variant affecting circulating vitamin D levels.

sources.name: Wang TJ et al. (2010) - Common genetic determinants of vitamin D insufficiency: a genome-wide association study

notes: GC (vitamin D binding protein) variant — one of the strongest genetic determinants of circulating 25(OH)D levels. Clinically relevant: carriers of certain alleles may need higher vitamin D supplementation. Effect is consistent across populations. Vitamin D levels also strongly depend on sun exposure and diet.

AA.description: Reduced CYP2R1 25-hydroxylase activity, lower circulating 25(OH)D levels

AG.description: Moderate CYP2R1 activity, intermediate vitamin D hydroxylation

GG.description: Full CYP2R1 activity, efficient conversion of vitamin D to 25(OH)D

description: CYP2R1 is the major vitamin D 25-hydroxylase that converts vitamin D to its circulating form 25(OH)D. Variants directly affect serum vitamin D levels.
notes: CYP2R1 performs the first hydroxylation step in vitamin D activation. GWAS confirmed it as one of the top genetic determinants of serum 25(OH)D levels, along with GC and DHCR7.

CC.description: Full DPD enzyme activity, standard 5-fluorouracil chemotherapy dosing appropriate

CT.description: ~50% DPD activity, increased risk of severe 5-FU toxicity, dose reduction recommended

TT.description: Complete DPD deficiency, 5-fluorouracil can cause life-threatening toxicity including neutropenia and mucositis

description: DPYD IVS14+1G>A (DPYD*2A) splice site variant eliminates dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity, causing potentially fatal toxicity from 5-fluorouracil and capecitabine chemotherapy.
notes: DPYD*2A causes exon 14 skipping. Carrier frequency ~1% in Europeans. DPD degrades >80% of administered 5-FU. Pre-treatment DPYD testing is now recommended/mandated in EU and increasingly in US. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Homozygous Lys262 reference; standard CYP2B6 enzyme activity. Bupropion, efavirenz, and cyclophosphamide are metabolized at expected rates, yielding typical plasma levels and standard therapeutic/toxicity profiles.

AG.description: Heterozygous Lys262Arg; one copy of the Arg262 variant moderately modulates CYP2B6 expression or activity. Intermediate metabolism of bupropion and cyclophosphamide with mild variation in plasma drug concentrations.

GG.description: Homozygous Arg262 variant; both CYP2B6 alleles carry the K262R substitution. Altered metabolism of substrates including bupropion (antidepressant/smoking cessation), efavirenz (HIV antiretroviral), and cyclophosphamide (chemotherapy/immunosuppressant), potentially shifting therapeutic windows.

description: CYP2B6 (cytochrome P450 family 2 subfamily B member 6) is the primary hepatic enzyme responsible for metabolism of bupropion to its active hydroxybupropion metabolite, efavirenz to its major phase I metabolite, and cyclophosphamide to its active alkylating species. The rs2279343 A>G variant encodes the Lys262Arg (K262R) amino acid substitution, which affects CYP2B6 protein expression and catalytic efficiency. This SNP defines part of the CYP2B6*4 haplotype and contributes to the well-documented inter-individual variability in CYP2B6-mediated drug metabolism.

sources.name: Hesse LM et al. (2004) - Influence of CYP2B6 genetic polymorphisms on drug metabolism

notes: G allele frequency is approximately 22–28% in European populations. CYP2B6 is one of the most polymorphic CYP enzymes, with multiple variants (CYP2B6*2 through *38) contributing to a 20-250-fold range of inter-individual activity. Clinical significance is highest for efavirenz (NNRTI for HIV) where poor metabolizers experience dose-related neurotoxicity and virologic response differences. Bupropion smoking-cessation efficacy may also vary by genotype. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: No *4 allele, standard CYP2C19 metabolizer status

AG.description: One *4 allele, reduced CYP2C19 function, may affect drug metabolism

GG.description: Homozygous *4, loss of CYP2C19 function, clopidogrel and PPI dose adjustment needed

description: CYP2C19*4 is a rare loss-of-function allele affecting a splice site. Contributes to poor metabolizer status for clopidogrel, proton pump inhibitors, and antidepressants.
notes: CYP2C19*4 is rare (<1% in most populations) but clinically significant. Together with *2 and *3, accounts for >95% of CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles worldwide. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2C19, severely impaired metabolism of clopidogrel, PPIs, and antidepressants

AG.description: One *3 allele, reduced CYP2C19 activity, consider alternative drugs or dose adjustment

GG.description: No *3 allele at this position, normal CYP2C19 metabolizer status

description: CYP2C19*3 introduces a premature stop codon (W212X), completely eliminating enzyme activity. Most common loss-of-function allele in East Asian populations.
notes: CYP2C19*3 is rare in Europeans (<1%) but found in 5-10% of East Asians. Combined with *2, these are the two most important CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles for clopidogrel pharmacogenomics. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Standard CYP2C19 metabolizer status, normal drug activation and metabolism

CT.description: Increased CYP2C19 activity, faster metabolism of PPIs and clopidogrel activation

TT.description: Significantly increased CYP2C19 activity, may need higher PPI doses, enhanced clopidogrel response

description: CYP2C19*17 promoter variant increases gene expression, producing ultra-rapid metabolism of proton pump inhibitors, antidepressants, and enhanced clopidogrel activation.
notes: *17 carriers metabolize PPIs too quickly, reducing efficacy. For clopidogrel, *17 enhances activation (beneficial). Frequency ~20-25% in Europeans, 3-5% in East Asians. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Homozygous Ile105 wild-type; GSTP1 enzyme maintains optimal substrate binding and standard detoxification activity toward chemotherapy agents including cyclophosphamide and platinum compounds.

AG.description: Heterozygous for the Val105 variant; intermediate detoxification capacity. Moderate reduction in enzyme thermostability and activity toward electrophilic chemotherapy substrates.

GG.description: Homozygous Val105 variant; substantially reduced GSTP1 enzyme activity and thermal stability. Impaired glutathione conjugation of cyclophosphamide, chlorambucil, and platinum-DNA adducts results in altered chemotherapy efficacy and toxicity profiles.

description: GSTP1 (glutathione S-transferase pi 1) catalyzes the conjugation of glutathione to electrophilic compounds as a Phase II detoxification mechanism. The rs1695 A>G substitution encodes the Ile105Val amino acid change, which destabilizes the H-site hydrophobic drug-binding pocket and reduces catalytic activity toward bulky aromatic substrates. This polymorphism modulates detoxification of many clinical chemotherapeutics — including alkylating agents, platinum compounds, and anthracyclines — altering both therapeutic response and organ toxicity patterns in cancer patients.

sources.name: Harries LW et al. (2001) - The effect of glutathione S-transferase genotype on outcomes in chemotherapy

notes: The G allele (Val105) frequency is approximately 32–38% in European populations and 16–24% in East Asian populations. GG individuals may exhibit altered toxicity profiles when treated with platinum-based regimens (cisplatin, oxaliplatin) or cyclophosphamide. GSTP1 activity reduction can increase oxidative stress burden during chemotherapy. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2C19 (*2/*2), clopidogrel ineffective, high risk of cardiovascular events on standard therapy

AG.description: Reduced CYP2C19 activity, diminished clopidogrel activation, consider alternative antiplatelet therapy

GG.description: Full CYP2C19 activity, normal clopidogrel activation and antiplatelet response

description: CYP2C19*2 is the most common loss-of-function allele, preventing activation of the prodrug clopidogrel. FDA black box warning mandates genotyping consideration before clopidogrel use.
notes: CYP2C19*2 aberrant splice site eliminates enzyme activity. 15-30% of most populations carry at least one *2 allele. FDA black box warning on clopidogrel labels regarding CYP2C19 poor metabolizers. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Reduced drug efflux, higher brain and tissue drug concentrations

AG.description: Moderate drug efflux pump function, intermediate drug levels

GG.description: Increased drug efflux, lower intracellular drug concentrations, potential treatment resistance

description: ABCB1/MDR1 C3435T variant affects P-glycoprotein expression, a key drug efflux transporter at the blood-brain barrier and intestinal epithelium affecting bioavailability of many drugs.
notes: P-glycoprotein transports hundreds of drugs including chemotherapeutics, antivirals, immunosuppressants, and cardiac drugs. The T allele (A) reduces P-gp expression, increasing drug absorption and brain penetration. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Normal CYP2D6 enzyme activity, standard drug metabolism

CT.description: Reduced CYP2D6 activity, may need dose adjustments for codeine, antidepressants

TT.description: Significantly reduced CYP2D6 activity, codeine ineffective, altered antidepressant response

description: CYP2D6*10 variant causes reduced enzyme activity affecting metabolism of ~25% of all drugs including codeine, tramadol, and many antidepressants.
notes: CYP2D6 metabolizes approximately 25% of clinically used drugs. The *10 allele (P34S) is especially common in East Asian populations (40-70%). Poor metabolizers cannot convert codeine to morphine. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (Gln141/Gln141); full ABCG2 protein function. Standard rosuvastatin and topotecan efflux activity — plasma drug levels fall within expected ranges.

AG.description: One copy of the Lys141 variant (Gln141Lys); ABCG2 surface expression and efflux activity are moderately reduced. Intermediate plasma concentrations of ABCG2 substrates including rosuvastatin are expected.

AA.description: Homozygous Lys141 variant; ABCG2 protein is substantially misfolded and degraded via ERAD, reducing membrane-level efflux. Plasma rosuvastatin concentrations are approximately 1.7-fold higher than in GG individuals, increasing both therapeutic effect and adverse event risk.

description: ABCG2 (ATP-binding cassette subfamily G member 2) is a half-transporter expressed at the intestinal epithelium, liver, kidney, and blood–brain barrier that mediates efflux of xenobiotics into luminal spaces. The rs2231142 G>A variant (Gln141Lys) reduces ABCG2 protein surface expression by approximately 50% through endoplasmic reticulum-associated degradation. Affected substrates include rosuvastatin (statin), topotecan (chemotherapy), methotrexate, sulfasalazine, and multiple tyrosine kinase inhibitors; the A allele is also the primary genetic determinant of gout susceptibility via reduced urate excretion.

sources.name: Morisaki K et al. (2008) - Single nucleotide polymorphisms modify the transporter activity of ABCG2

notes: The A allele (Lys141) reaches ~30% frequency in East Asian populations versus ~6–9% in Europeans. Clinical implications extend beyond rosuvastatin: this variant significantly affects topotecan bioavailability in cancer chemotherapy regimens and oral bioavailability of sulfasalazine for inflammatory bowel disease. Gout associations are driven by reduced renal urate secretion. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

GG.description: Standard CYP2B6 activity, normal efavirenz and methadone metabolism

GT.description: Reduced CYP2B6 activity, slightly elevated drug levels, may need dose adjustment

TT.description: Significantly reduced CYP2B6 activity, high risk of efavirenz CNS toxicity, slow methadone metabolism

description: CYP2B6*6 (Q172H + K262R) is the most common reduced-function allele. Critical for metabolism of efavirenz (HIV), methadone (opioid maintenance), and bupropion (smoking cessation).
notes: CYP2B6*6 frequency ~25% globally, up to 50% in some African populations. TT genotype causes efavirenz plasma levels 3-4x higher than GG, increasing CNS side effects (vivid dreams, dizziness). Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Normal bilirubin glucuronidation, standard irinotecan metabolism

AT.description: Mildly reduced glucuronidation, slightly elevated bilirubin and irinotecan toxicity risk

TT.description: Significantly reduced UGT1A1 activity, elevated bilirubin (Gilbert syndrome), high irinotecan toxicity risk

description: UGT1A1*28 TA repeat variant reduces UGT1A1 expression, causing Gilbert syndrome (benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia) and increasing irinotecan chemotherapy toxicity risk.
notes: UGT1A1*28 (7 TA repeats vs 6 in *1) reduces transcription by ~70%. Gilbert syndrome affects 5-10% of population. FDA label for irinotecan recommends UGT1A1 genotyping. *28/*28 patients need 30% dose reduction. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AG.description: One non-functional CYP2D6 allele, reduced metabolism of CYP2D6 substrates

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2D6 (*4/*4), unable to metabolize many common drugs including codeine, tamoxifen

GG.description: Normal CYP2D6 enzyme function, standard drug metabolism

description: CYP2D6*4 is the most common non-functional allele in Europeans, causing a splicing defect that eliminates enzyme activity. Affects metabolism of ~25% of prescription drugs.
notes: CYP2D6*4 (G>A at intron 3/exon 4 boundary) is the most common PM allele in Europeans (20-25%). Homozygotes are poor metabolizers (~7% of Europeans). FDA recommends CYP2D6 testing for multiple drugs. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Impaired hepatic statin uptake, 15-20x increased risk of simvastatin-induced myopathy

CT.description: Reduced hepatic statin uptake, ~5x increased risk of statin myopathy

TT.description: Normal OATP1B1 transporter function, standard statin myopathy risk

description: SLCO1B1*5 (V174A) reduces hepatic uptake of statins via the OATP1B1 transporter, increasing plasma statin levels and risk of myopathy. FDA-relevant pharmacogenomic marker.
notes: SLCO1B1*5 carrier frequency ~15% in Europeans. The SEARCH trial showed CC genotype had 16.9% vs 0.6% myopathy rate on simvastatin 80mg. CPIC recommends avoiding simvastatin 80mg in C allele carriers. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Full CYP3A5 expression, rapid tacrolimus metabolism, may need higher doses

CT.description: Reduced CYP3A5 expression, intermediate tacrolimus metabolism

TT.description: No CYP3A5 expression (cryptic splice site), slower tacrolimus metabolism, lower doses needed

description: CYP3A5*3 splice variant determines CYP3A5 expression. Critical for transplant immunosuppression dosing — tacrolimus dose requirements differ 1.5-2x between expressors and non-expressors.
notes: *3 creates a cryptic splice site that abolishes CYP3A5 expression. ~90% of Europeans are *3/*3 non-expressors, vs ~30% of Africans. CPIC recommends genotype-guided tacrolimus dosing in transplant patients. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Normal thiopurine methyltransferase function, standard dosing appropriate

CT.description: Reduced TPMT enzyme stability, dose reduction recommended for thiopurines

TT.description: Severely reduced TPMT activity, high risk of life-threatening toxicity at standard thiopurine doses

description: TPMT*3B component (Y240C) causes enzyme instability and rapid degradation. Part of the TPMT*3A haplotype when combined with *3C, the most common deficient allele in Europeans.
notes: TPMT*3A (combination of *3B and *3C) is the most common deficiency allele in Europeans (~5%). The Y240C substitution causes misfolding and rapid proteasomal degradation of the enzyme. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Full TPMT enzyme activity, standard azathioprine/6-MP dosing appropriate

AG.description: Reduced TPMT activity, dose reduction recommended to prevent myelosuppression

GG.description: Very low TPMT activity, high risk of life-threatening myelosuppression at standard doses

description: TPMT*3C variant reduces thiopurine methyltransferase activity. Critical for dosing azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine, and thioguanine to prevent potentially fatal bone marrow toxicity.
notes: TPMT deficiency affects ~0.3% of population (homozygous) and ~10% are intermediate metabolizers. FDA mandates TPMT testing before thiopurine therapy. TPMT*3C is the most common variant in East Asian and African populations. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (GG haplotype H7/H7); VKORC1 is expressed at high levels. Higher warfarin doses are typically required to achieve therapeutic anticoagulation (INR 2–3). Common in African-ancestry individuals.

AG.description: Heterozygous AG; intermediate VKORC1 expression. Moderate warfarin dose required. This genotype is the most common in European populations and requires individualized dose adjustment.

AA.description: Homozygous A allele (haplotype H1/H1); VKORC1 promoter activity is markedly reduced, lowering enzyme expression. Significantly lower warfarin doses needed — typically 30–40% below average. AA individuals face substantially elevated bleeding risk if dosed at standard levels.

description: VKORC1 (vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1) catalyzes the reduction of vitamin K epoxide back to the active hydroquinone form required for activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. The rs9934438 G>A variant is an intronic SNP that tags the H1 VKORC1 haplotype, which is associated with a -1639G>A promoter variant reducing VKORC1 transcription by ~40%. This is one of the two primary genetic determinants of warfarin dose (alongside CYP2C9), and is incorporated into FDA pharmacogenomic dosing guidelines for warfarin, acenocoumarol, and phenprocoumon.

sources.name: Rieder MJ et al. (2005) - Effect of VKORC1 haplotypes on transcriptional regulation and warfarin dose

notes: A allele frequency ranges from ~9% in African populations to ~37% in Europeans to ~93% in East Asians — this extreme population differentiation explains why East Asian patients require substantially lower warfarin doses on average. Algorithms such as IWPC and WarfarinDosing.org incorporate this variant alongside CYP2C9 genotype and clinical factors for precise dosing. Also affects sensitivity to acenocoumarol and phenprocoumon (coumarin anticoagulants). This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Low VKORC1 sensitivity, requires higher warfarin doses (5-7 mg/day typical)

CT.description: Moderate VKORC1 sensitivity, intermediate warfarin dose requirement (3-5 mg/day typical)

TT.description: High VKORC1 sensitivity, requires lower warfarin doses (1.5-3 mg/day typical), higher bleeding risk at standard doses

description: VKORC1 -1639G>A promoter variant is the single strongest predictor of warfarin dose requirement, accounting for ~25% of dose variability. Affects vitamin K epoxide reductase expression.
notes: VKORC1 genotype is the strongest single predictor of warfarin dose. The T allele reduces VKORC1 expression, making patients more sensitive to warfarin. Very common in East Asian populations (>90% TT). Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Normal CYP2C9*1 activity, standard warfarin dosing

CT.description: Intermediate CYP2C9 activity (~60%), may require lower warfarin dose

TT.description: Poor CYP2C9 activity (~30%), significantly lower warfarin dose needed

description: CYP2C9*2 variant (R144C) reduces warfarin metabolism. Combined with CYP2C9*3 and VKORC1 status, guides pharmacogenomic anticoagulant dosing.
notes: CYP2C9*2 reduces enzyme activity by ~30-40%. Common in European populations (10-15%). Together with *3 and VKORC1 genotype, accounts for ~40% of warfarin dose variability. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Normal CYP2C9 enzyme activity, standard warfarin dosing appropriate

AC.description: Reduced CYP2C9 activity (~50%), lower warfarin dose may be needed

CC.description: Significantly reduced CYP2C9 activity, substantially lower warfarin dose required

description: CYP2C9*3 variant (I359L) causes significantly reduced metabolism of warfarin and other CYP2C9 substrates. Critical pharmacogenomic marker for anticoagulant dosing.
notes: CYP2C9*3 allele reduces enzyme activity by ~80%. Combined with VKORC1 genotyping, enables pharmacogenomic warfarin dosing. FDA-recommended testing before warfarin initiation. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Smooth chin

CT.description: Mild chin cleft

TT.description: Prominent cleft chin

description: Variation in jaw bone fusion creating a visible Y-shaped dimple in the chin.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Associated with cleft chin morphology in consumer genetics studies. Limited peer-reviewed evidence. Facial morphology is highly polygenic and influenced by developmental environment. Treat prediction with caution.

TT.description: Free (detached) earlobes

CC.description: Attached earlobes

description: Ectodysplasin receptor variant influencing earlobe attachment pattern.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2015) - A genome-wide association study identifies multiple loci for variation in human ear morphology

notes: EDAR region variant associated with earlobe attachment in multi-ethnic GWAS. Earlobe morphology is polygenic — not the simple dominant/recessive trait described in textbooks. Multiple loci contribute to the continuous spectrum of earlobe attachment.

AA.description: No cheek dimples

AG.description: Likely to have dimples on one or both cheeks

GG.description: Strong tendency for cheek dimples

description: Facial muscle variation creating visible indentations in cheeks when smiling.

sources.name: Shaffer JR et al. (2016) - Genome-wide association study reveals multiple loci influencing normal human facial morphology

notes: Weakly associated with cheek dimples in consumer genetics datasets. Evidence is limited to proprietary GWAS from direct-to-consumer companies; no peer-reviewed replication in independent cohorts. Dimple genetics remain poorly understood.

GG.description: Very high likelihood of brown eyes (>95%)

AG.description: Intermediate pigmentation, likely green or hazel eyes (70-80%)

AA.description: Very high likelihood of blue eyes (>90%)

description: Primary determinant of eye color variation in Europeans. This SNP in the HERC2 gene regulates OCA2 expression, controlling melanin production in the iris.

sources.name: Sturm RA et al. (2008) - A single SNP in an evolutionary conserved region within intron 86 of the HERC2 gene determines human blue-brown eye color

notes: Strongest single genetic predictor of eye color in Europeans, explaining ~75% of blue vs brown variation. Effect is weaker in non-European populations where brown eyes are nearly universal. Works in concert with OCA2 and other pigmentation loci.

CC.description: Normal melanin production, contributes to darker eye color

CT.description: Slightly reduced melanin, may lighten eye color

TT.description: Reduced melanin production, contributes to lighter eye colors

description: OCA2 gene variant that modifies eye color by affecting melanin production. Works in combination with rs12913832.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2007) - A three-single-nucleotide polymorphism haplotype in intron 1 of OCA2 explains most human eye-color variation

notes: Modifier of eye color that works alongside rs12913832 (HERC2). Most impactful in individuals with intermediate pigmentation — can shift hazel eyes toward blue or green. Effect size is moderate compared to the primary HERC2 variant.

GG.description: Higher melanin, contributes to darker eye colors

TT.description: Lower melanin, contributes to lighter eye colors (especially green)

description: SLC24A4 gene variant associated with green eye color variation. Secondary modifier of eye pigmentation.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: Contributing locus for eye color identified in European GWAS. Has a smaller effect than HERC2/OCA2 variants but adds predictive power in multi-SNP models. Also weakly associated with hair color variation.

AA.description: Contributes to darker eye pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate eye pigmentation effect

GG.description: Contributes to lighter eye colors (blue/green)

description: Additional OCA2 region variant modifying eye color. Secondary modifier with smaller effect than rs12913832.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2007) - A three-single-nucleotide polymorphism haplotype in intron 1 of OCA2 explains most human eye-color variation

notes: Part of a three-SNP OCA2 haplotype associated with blue/brown eye color. Most studied in European-descent populations. Works additively with HERC2 rs12913832 to fine-tune iris pigmentation.

AA.description: Reduced tyrosinase activity, contributes to lighter/bluer eye color

AG.description: Intermediate tyrosinase function, subtle influence toward lighter eye color

GG.description: Normal tyrosinase activity at this position, standard melanin contribution to eye color

description: TYR (tyrosinase) variant modifies eye color by affecting the rate-limiting enzyme in melanin biosynthesis. Contributes to the blue eye phenotype in combination with HERC2/OCA2.
notes: Tyrosinase catalyzes the first two steps of melanin synthesis. This variant is one of several TYR polymorphisms affecting pigmentation. Also associated with freckling and tanning response.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; TYRP1 activity is standard. Brown pigmentation expression is at baseline levels without the A-allele modifier effect.

CA.description: One copy of the A allele provides partial TYRP1 activity modulation. Eye color is influenced in an additive direction toward deeper or lighter brown depending on other pigmentation loci.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; both copies carry the A allele associated with modified TYRP1 enzymatic activity. In European populations, the A allele reaches ~66% frequency, influencing brown-eye shade and saturation.

description: TYRP1 (tyrosinase-related protein 1) encodes an enzyme involved in the eumelanin synthesis pathway, oxidizing 5,6-dihydroxyindole-2-carboxylic acid (DHICA) during melanin production. The rs683 variant modulates TYRP1 activity and acts as a modifier of brown eye color intensity, particularly in concert with OCA2 and HERC2 variants. Population studies demonstrate strong European enrichment of the A allele (~66%), with very low frequency in East Asians (~0.4%).

sources.name: Kayser M et al. (2008) - Three genome-wide association studies and a linkage analysis identify HERC2 as a human iris color gene

notes: The A allele frequency varies dramatically across ancestries: ~66% in Europeans, ~0.4% in East Asians, ~18% in Africans. This extreme population stratification means eye color predictions using this variant are most informative in European-ancestry individuals. Pronounced population-frequency differences make this a useful ancestry-informative marker. Not clinically actionable; cosmetic trait only.

AA.description: Modified OCA2 expression favoring green eye color, particularly in light-eyed individuals

AG.description: Intermediate OCA2 modification, subtle influence toward green eye tones

GG.description: Standard OCA2 expression at this position, baseline eye color contribution

description: OCA2 regulatory variant that modifies eye color, particularly associated with green eye color phenotype. Works as part of the multi-gene eye color determination system.
notes: Green eye color is genetically complex, involving multiple loci. This OCA2 variant contributes to the green eye phenotype alongside HERC2, IRF4, and other pigmentation genes. Green eyes are found in ~2% of the global population.

AA.description: Reduced OCA2/HERC2 expression, lighter iris pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate melanin in iris, intermediate eye color intensity

GG.description: Higher OCA2 expression, more intense iris pigmentation

description: OCA2/HERC2 regulatory variant affecting melanin density in the iris. Part of the multi-SNP eye color determination system on chromosome 15.
notes: Works in concert with rs12913832 (HERC2) to determine overall eye color intensity. The OCA2/HERC2 region on 15q contains the strongest eye color determinants.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; this HERC2 regulatory region allele is associated with reduced OCA2 promoter activity, favoring lighter eye colors. European populations show ~78% A allele frequency.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate effect on HERC2-mediated OCA2 regulation. Eye color is likely hazel, green, or light brown depending on compound genotype across other pigmentation loci.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; the G allele is enriched in African (~63-70%) and East Asian (~69-73%) populations and is associated with higher OCA2 expression and darker iris pigmentation.

description: rs2238289 lies within the HERC2 gene (chromosome 15) and functions as a cis-regulatory variant modulating the promoter activity of the adjacent OCA2 (P protein) gene, which encodes a melanosomal transporter critical for melanin synthesis. Multiple HERC2 intronic variants, including rs2238289, form haplotypes that finely tune OCA2 expression levels in melanocytes. The A allele predominates in Europeans (~78%) while the G allele predominates in African and East Asian populations.

sources.name: Sturm RA et al. (2008) - A single SNP in an evolutionary conserved region within intron 86 of the HERC2 gene determines human blue-brown eye color

notes: This variant works synergistically with rs12913832 (the canonical HERC2 eye color SNP). European populations show very high A allele frequency (~78%), while African and East Asian populations predominantly carry G (~63-73%). Eye color predictions are most accurate when a multi-SNP panel spanning HERC2, OCA2, and other loci is used. Not clinically actionable; cosmetic pigmentation trait.

AA.description: Thinner eyebrows

AG.description: Medium eyebrow thickness

GG.description: Thicker/bushier eyebrows

description: Forkhead transcription factor variant influencing eyebrow hair density and thickness.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Associated with eyebrow thickness in admixed Latin American populations. Effect may vary across ethnic groups. Hair morphology traits are polygenic with substantial environmental influence (grooming habits).

TT.description: Fewer freckles

GG.description: Dense freckling tendency

description: Interferon regulatory factor variant influencing melanocyte-driven freckle formation.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: IRF4 regulatory variant associated with freckle density and sun sensitivity in European populations. Pleiotropic effects include contributions to hair color and melanoma risk. Effect size is moderate; freckling is influenced by many loci plus UV exposure.

GG.description: Normal MC1R function, lower freckling tendency

GT.description: Increased freckling tendency, especially with sun exposure

TT.description: Strong freckling tendency, many freckles likely

description: MC1R variant (D84E) strongly associated with freckling, even in non-redheads. Also associated with fair skin and sun sensitivity.

sources.name: Bastiaens M et al. (2001) - Association of the melanocortin-1 receptor gene with freckling and solar lentigines in children

notes: MC1R variant (V60L) associated with freckling and fair skin but rarely causes red hair alone. One of the milder MC1R variants. Freckling tendency is polygenic — MC1R explains the largest fraction but sun exposure is a major environmental modifier.

CC.description: Higher melanin production, darker hair color

CT.description: Intermediate melanin, light brown or dark blond hair

TT.description: Reduced melanin, increased likelihood of blond hair in Europeans

description: KITLG gene variant strongly associated with blond hair in European populations. Each T allele increases blond hair likelihood.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: Associated with blond hair in Europeans via regulation of KITLG expression in hair follicles. Effect is population-specific — rare outside European-descent populations. Explains only a fraction of blond hair variation; many other loci contribute.

CC.description: Normal MC1R function, no red hair from this variant

CT.description: Carrier of red hair variant, may have subtle red tints or increased freckling

TT.description: High likelihood of red or strawberry blonde hair (80-90%)

description: Most common MC1R variant associated with red hair. Loss of function mutation in melanocortin receptor, shifting eumelanin to pheomelanin production.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: One of the strongest MC1R red hair variants (R151C). Highly penetrant in homozygotes — ~80% chance of red hair. Also increases melanoma risk independently of hair color. Most common in Northern European populations (Ireland, Scotland).

CC.description: Normal MC1R function

CT.description: Carrier status, possible subtle red tones

TT.description: High likelihood of red or auburn hair (75-85%)

description: Another common MC1R red hair variant (R160W mutation). Often found in combination with other MC1R variants.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (R160W) associated with red hair, though less penetrant than R151C. Compound heterozygosity with other MC1R variants increases red hair probability. Also linked to increased sun sensitivity and freckling.

AA.description: Straight hair tendency

TT.description: Curly hair tendency

description: Trichohyalin protein variant affecting hair fiber shape and curliness.

sources.name: Medland SE et al. (2009) - Common variants in the trichohyalin gene are associated with straight hair in Europeans

notes: TCHH variant strongly associated with straight vs curly hair in Europeans. Effect is specific to European populations where the variant is common (~10% frequency). Hair curliness is highly polygenic — this SNP captures only part of the variation.

TT.description: Typical European/African hair characteristics, thinner hair strands

CT.description: Thicker hair strands, straighter hair

CC.description: Very thick hair strands, very straight hair (common in East Asians)

description: EDAR gene variant (370A allele) prevalent in East Asian populations. Affects hair follicle development, leading to thicker, straighter hair shafts.

sources.name: Fujimoto A et al. (2008) - A scan for genetic determinants of human hair morphology: EDAR is associated with Asian hair thickness

notes: EDAR V370A variant at near-fixation in East Asian and Native American populations but rare elsewhere. Affects hair thickness, tooth morphology, sweat gland density, and breast tissue. One of the strongest examples of recent positive selection in humans.

CC.description: Normal melanin production, darker hair and skin

CT.description: Intermediate pigmentation levels

TT.description: Reduced melanin, lighter hair (especially blond/red) and skin, more freckling

description: IRF4 transcription factor variant affecting melanocyte function. Associated with lighter hair, skin, and increased freckling.

sources.name: Han J et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin pigmentation

notes: IRF4 variant with pleiotropic effects on skin, hair, and eye pigmentation. Strongly associated with freckling, sun sensitivity, and lighter hair color in Europeans. Also linked to melanoma susceptibility independently of pigmentation.

TT.description: Each T allele adds ~0.1cm to head circumference on average

CT.description: Intermediate head circumference contribution

CC.description: Neutral or slightly reduced head circumference

description: HMGA2 variant associated with head circumference variation. Same gene region affects overall height. Small individual effect.

sources.name: Ikram MA et al. (2012) - Common variants at 6q22 and 17q21 are associated with intracranial volume

notes: Associated with intracranial volume and head circumference in large GWAS meta-analyses. Effect size is small. Head circumference is highly polygenic and influenced by nutrition and prenatal environment. Not clinically predictive for any individual.

TT.description: Each T allele adds ~0.4cm to height on average

CT.description: Intermediate height contribution

CC.description: Neutral or slightly reduced height contribution

description: HMGA2 gene variant, one of the strongest single-SNP associations with adult height. However, height is polygenic with 700+ variants.

sources.name: Weedon MN et al. (2007) - A common variant of HMGA2 is associated with adult and childhood height in the general population

notes: HMGA2 variant associated with height variation, though effect size is small (~0.3-0.4 cm per allele). Height is extremely polygenic (thousands of variants). This is one of the more replicated individual loci but explains <0.1% of height variation.

AA.description: Minor positive contribution to height (~0.3cm per allele)

AG.description: Intermediate height contribution

GG.description: Neutral or slightly reduced height contribution

description: GDF5 growth factor variant associated with height and osteoarthritis. Small effect size as height is highly polygenic.

sources.name: Sanna S et al. (2008) - Common variants in the GDF5-UQCC region are associated with variation in human height

notes: GDF5 variant associated with height and osteoarthritis risk. Small effect on height (~0.2 cm per allele). Also implicated in joint development and skeletal morphology. Height prediction requires hundreds of SNPs for meaningful accuracy.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; SLC45A2 Glu272 variant associated with normal/higher melanin production. Most common in African (~95%) and South Asian (~80%) populations.

CT.description: Heterozygous; one copy of the derived T allele (Lys272) associated with intermediate SLC45A2 activity. Pigmentation level is intermediate between darker and lighter extremes.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; the T allele (p.Glu272Lys) is strongly enriched in East Asian populations (~36-39%) and associated with lighter skin pigmentation adaptation. Lower frequency in Europeans (~1-2%) and Africans (~5%).

description: SLC45A2 (membrane-associated transporter protein, MATP) is a melanosomal transporter involved in melanin synthesis regulation. The rs26722 variant encodes a missense change (Glu272Lys) that modulates transporter function and melanin output. Unlike the European skin-lightening allele at SLC24A5, the SLC45A2 rs26722 T allele shows highest frequency in East Asian populations (~37%), reflecting a distinct adaptive pigmentation lineage. The variant exemplifies convergent evolution of reduced pigmentation across geographically separated populations.

sources.name: Graf J et al. (2007) - Single nucleotide polymorphisms in the MATP gene are associated with normal human pigmentation variation

notes: Strong population stratification: T allele ~37% in East Asians and Koreans, ~1-2% in Europeans, ~5% in Africans. This suggests the light-skin adaptation in East Asian populations used SLC45A2 as one mechanism, while European populations primarily used SLC24A5 rs1426654 — a classic case of convergent evolution. Not clinically actionable; cosmetic pigmentation trait. Ancestry-informative for East Asian vs. other populations.

AA.description: Increased androgen receptor sensitivity, higher risk of male pattern baldness

AG.description: Intermediate risk (only in females, males have one copy on X chromosome)

GG.description: Reduced androgen receptor sensitivity, lower baldness risk

description: Androgen receptor gene variant on X chromosome. Males inherit from mother. Affects sensitivity to DHT, primary driver of male pattern baldness.

sources.name: Hillmer AM et al. (2005) - Genetic variation in the human androgen receptor gene is the major determinant of common early-onset androgenetic alopecia

notes: Androgen receptor variant associated with male pattern baldness risk. One of multiple contributing loci — androgenetic alopecia is polygenic. The X-linked pattern means maternal family history is more informative. Environmental factors (stress, nutrition) also play a role.

AA.description: Lower baldness risk

CC.description: Higher male pattern baldness risk

description: Histone deacetylase variant associated with androgenetic alopecia susceptibility.

sources.name: Richards JB et al. (2008) - Male-pattern baldness susceptibility locus at 20p11

notes: Associated with androgenetic alopecia at the 20p11 locus. Effect is moderate — male pattern baldness involves multiple loci including AR (X-linked) and this autosomal variant. Prediction accuracy improves substantially with multi-SNP models.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard TYR enzyme activity. No increased risk for oculocutaneous albinism type 1 (OCA1). Most common genotype globally (~75-87% depending on ancestry).

TC.description: Heterozygous carrier of the C allele, which encodes a tyrosinase variant with reduced but not absent activity. Carriers typically display normal or slightly lighter pigmentation without clinical albinism.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; substantially reduced TYR enzymatic activity. In combination with other TYR variants, homozygous CC is associated with oculocutaneous albinism type 1 (OCA1). The C allele frequency is ~22% in African populations and ~5-7% in Europeans.

description: TYR encodes tyrosinase, the rate-limiting enzyme in melanin biosynthesis, catalyzing the oxidation of L-tyrosine to DOPA and DOPA-quinone. The rs2240203 variant results in an amino acid change that reduces tyrosinase enzymatic activity in a dose-dependent manner. Compound heterozygosity involving this and other TYR pathogenic variants underlies many OCA1 cases. This SNP is classified as a hypomorphic allele rather than a fully null mutation.

sources.name: Hutton SM & Spritz RA (2008) - Comprehensive analysis of oculocutaneous albinism among non-Hispanic caucasians shows that OCA1 is the most prevalent OCA type

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. A CC genotype alone is not sufficient to diagnose OCA1 — clinical albinism typically requires compound heterozygosity with a second pathogenic TYR variant. Discuss with a healthcare provider or genetic counselor if concerned. Allele frequencies show strong population stratification: C allele ~22% in Africans vs ~5-7% in Europeans. The C allele is a hypomorphic variant, not a null allele, and its clinical significance depends heavily on the compound genotype.

CC.phenotype: No Red Hair from This Variant
CC.description: Normal MC1R signaling at this position, eumelanin production unaffected

CT.description: One copy of D84E, slight shift toward pheomelanin, may lighten hair color

TT.description: Homozygous D84E, reduced eumelanin switching, contributes to red hair and fair skin phenotype

description: MC1R D84E is a loss-of-function variant reducing melanocortin-1 receptor signaling, shifting melanin production from eumelanin (brown/black) to pheomelanin (red/yellow).
notes: MC1R D84E is one of several MC1R variants contributing to red hair phenotype. Usually requires compound heterozygosity with another MC1R variant (R151C, R160W, D294H) for full red hair expression.

AA.description: Reduced melanin production, light skin pigmentation (European-type)

AG.description: Intermediate melanin levels, medium skin tone

GG.description: Normal melanin production, darker skin pigmentation

description: Major determinant of light skin in European and South Asian populations. The A allele (derived) reduces melanin ~30-40%.

sources.name: Lamason RL et al. (2005) - SLC24A5, a putative cation exchanger, affects pigmentation in zebrafish and humans

notes: Major determinant of light skin in Europeans and South Asians. The derived A allele is nearly fixed in European populations and at high frequency in South Asia. One of the strongest known examples of convergent evolution for light skin pigmentation.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; associated with lower propensity for freckling and lighter pigmentation saturation in sun-exposed skin. Most common in European populations (~34% G allele frequency).

GA.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate freckling and pigmentation saturation. The A allele at ~66% global frequency reflects derived European enrichment for modified BNC2 regulation.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with increased tendency to develop freckles and higher skin pigmentation saturation in UV-exposed areas. The A allele predominates in European (~66%) populations.

description: BNC2 (basonuclin 2) encodes a zinc-finger transcription factor expressed in keratinocytes and melanocytes. Genome-wide association studies have identified BNC2 variants, including rs10756819, as associated with freckling propensity and pigmentation saturation traits. The mechanism involves BNC2 regulation of melanocyte differentiation and UV-response gene networks. The A allele shows notable enrichment in European populations (~66%) relative to African populations (~16%).

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Significant population stratification: A allele ~66% in Europeans, ~16% in Africans, ~54% in East Asians. The freckling association is strongest in individuals of Northern European descent who have lower baseline melanin. Freckling reflects a UV-response phenotype and is considered a surrogate marker of cumulative sun damage risk. Not a direct melanoma predictor; UV protection habits remain the primary preventive measure.

AA.description: Lower melanin production, lighter skin pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate melanin, intermediate skin tone

GG.description: Higher melanin production, darker skin pigmentation

description: SLC45A2 L374F variant strongly associated with skin pigmentation differences across populations.
notes: L374F variant in SLC45A2 melanin transporter gene. Major effect in European populations. The A allele frequency is >95% in Europeans, <5% in sub-Saharan Africans.

GG.description: Reduced melanin, lighter skin and eyes (European-type)

CC.description: Normal melanin production, darker skin and eyes

description: SLC45A2 variant affecting melanin synthesis. The G allele is nearly fixed in Europeans and contributes to light skin and eyes.

sources.name: Graf J et al. (2005) - Single nucleotide polymorphisms in the MATP gene are associated with normal human pigmentation variation

notes: SLC45A2 variant strongly associated with light skin and eye color in Europeans. Nearly fixed for the derived allele in Europeans but rare in African and East Asian populations. Has a stronger effect on skin color than eye color.

CC.description: Separated eyebrows

CT.description: Some eyebrow connectivity

TT.description: Tendency for connected eyebrows (unibrow)

description: Transcription factor variant influencing eyebrow hair growth pattern and density.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: PAX3 variant associated with unibrow (synophrys) tendency, identified in a Latin American admixed population GWAS. Effect may differ across populations. Eyebrow morphology is influenced by multiple genetic and hormonal factors.

GG.description: Straight hairline

GA.description: Subtle V-shaped hairline

AA.description: Distinct widow's peak hairline

description: Characteristic V-shaped point in the center of the hairline.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Linked to widow's peak hairline in facial morphology GWAS. The genetic architecture of hairline shape is complex and not well characterized. Limited evidence for this specific SNP-trait association.

AA.description: LIN28B variant associated with later onset of puberty and menarche by several months

AC.description: Intermediate LIN28B effect on pubertal timing

CC.description: Standard LIN28B expression, tendency toward earlier menarche onset

description: LIN28B variant affects pubertal timing through regulation of the let-7 microRNA family. LIN28B controls the transition from juvenile to adult gene expression programs.
notes: LIN28B was among the first GWAS hits for menarche timing. Each allele shifts menarche by ~0.1-0.2 years. LIN28B also affects adult height through its role in growth tempo regulation.

AA.description: Increased aromatase activity, higher estradiol production from androgens

AG.description: Moderate aromatase activity, intermediate estrogen synthesis

GG.description: Reduced aromatase activity, lower estrogen conversion from androgens

description: CYP19A1 (aromatase) variant affects the enzyme that converts androgens to estrogens. Critical for reproductive function, bone health, and breast cancer risk.
notes: Aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole) are first-line endocrine therapy for ER+ breast cancer. CYP19A1 variants may affect response to these drugs and natural estrogen levels.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard CYP17A1-associated vascular tone and blood pressure regulation. Most individuals globally (~91%) carry this genotype.

TC.description: Heterozygous; one C allele associated with a modest increase in systolic blood pressure (~1-2 mmHg). The effect is small but measurable at the population level and contributes to hypertension polygenic risk.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; C allele (~9% globally, ~27-29% East Asian) associated with the highest blood pressure elevation from this locus. Strongest effect in East Asian populations where C allele frequency is substantially higher.

description: CYP17A1 (5'-nucleotidase, cytosolic II) encodes an enzyme involved in nucleotide metabolism and purine recycling. The rs11191548 variant near CYP17A1 on chromosome 10q24 was identified in large GWAS studies as associated with systolic and diastolic blood pressure. The mechanism may involve altered purinergic signaling in vascular smooth muscle cells, since adenosine — a product of CYP17A1 activity — is a potent vasodilator. Higher CYP17A1 activity associated with the C allele could reduce extracellular adenosine, promoting vasoconstriction and elevated blood pressure.

sources.name: Newton-Cheh C et al. (2009) - Genome-wide association study identifies eight loci associated with blood pressure

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The C allele shows strong population stratification: ~9% globally, ~8-9% European, ~4-5% African, ~27-29% East Asian. The per-allele blood pressure effect is ~1-1.5 mmHg systolic — modest individually but significant at population scale and in conjunction with other hypertension risk alleles. Hypertension is highly polygenic and heavily influenced by diet (sodium intake), physical activity, and body weight. Individuals with the C allele and family history of hypertension or cardiovascular disease should discuss blood pressure monitoring and lifestyle optimization with their physician.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; baseline CYP19A1 (aromatase) expression and estrogen synthesis from androgens. Standard circulating estradiol and estrone levels in both sexes (~51% G globally).

GA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate CYP19A1 expression level. Estrogen levels are between GG and AA extremes. May influence estrogen-dependent phenotypes such as bone mineral density and menopausal symptom severity.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; A allele (~49% globally, ~51% European) associated with modified CYP19A1 expression and altered estradiol/estrone ratios. Associated in some studies with altered bone density, hormone-sensitive cancer risk, and reproductive phenotypes.

description: CYP19A1 encodes aromatase, the enzyme responsible for the final step in estrogen biosynthesis — converting androgens (testosterone, androstenedione) to estrogens (estradiol, estrone). rs10046 is a 3'UTR variant that influences CYP19A1 expression levels, affecting circulating estradiol concentrations in both sexes. Estrogen plays critical roles in bone density maintenance, cardiovascular protection, reproductive function, and breast/endometrial tissue proliferation. Variations at this locus have been associated with estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer risk, endometriosis, bone mineral density, and hormone replacement therapy outcomes.

sources.name: Haiman CA et al. (2007) - A common genetic variant in the CYP19A1 locus is associated with postmenopausal estrogen levels

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The G and A alleles are nearly equally common (~50/50) globally, with European populations showing ~51% A frequency. The association with estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer risk from this locus confers modest effect sizes (OR ~1.05-1.15 per allele); it is one of dozens of breast cancer susceptibility loci. Bone density associations are more robust. Individuals with AA genotype and family history of estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer or osteoporosis should discuss screening intervals and hormone therapy decisions with their physician.

AA.description: More sensitive FSH receptor, lower FSH needed for follicular response, better IVF response

AG.description: Moderate FSH receptor sensitivity, standard ovarian response to gonadotropins

GG.description: Less sensitive FSH receptor, higher FSH levels needed, may need higher IVF gonadotropin doses

description: FSHR Asn680Ser variant affects FSH receptor sensitivity in ovarian granulosa cells. Determines ovarian response to gonadotropins and is critical for IVF protocol optimization.
notes: Ser680 variant increases FSH threshold for follicular recruitment. Clinically used to personalize IVF gonadotropin dosing — Ser/Ser women typically need higher doses. Also affects age at menopause.

CC.description: Modified OR7D4 receptor, androstenone perceived as pleasant or undetectable
