CT.description: Mixed perception, androstenone has intermediate intensity

TT.description: Fully functional OR7D4, androstenone perceived as intensely unpleasant 'sweaty/urinous'

description: OR7D4 olfactory receptor variant determines perception of androstenone, a steroid pheromone found in pork (boar taint), sweat, and truffles. Classic example of human olfactory genetic variation.
notes: OR7D4 R88W variant dramatically alters androstenone perception. ~50% of people find androstenone offensive, ~35% cannot smell it, and ~15% find it pleasant — largely determined by OR7D4 genotype.

AA.description: Homozygous PTPN22 1858T, strongly increased risk of multiple autoimmune diseases

AG.description: PTPN22 1858T carrier, ~1.5-2x increased risk of type 1 diabetes, RA, and thyroid autoimmunity

GG.description: Wild-type PTPN22, population-average autoimmune disease susceptibility

description: PTPN22 R620W is the strongest non-HLA autoimmune risk variant. Affects T-cell receptor signaling and negative regulation of T-cell activation, increasing susceptibility to multiple autoimmune diseases.
notes: PTPN22 1858T is a gain-of-function variant that increases phosphatase activity, reducing T-cell activation threshold. Shared risk variant for type 1 diabetes, RA, SLE, thyroid autoimmunity, and vitiligo.

AA.description: Non-functional OR5A1 receptor, unable to detect violet/berry aroma (beta-ionone)

AG.description: Intermediate OR5A1 function, can detect beta-ionone at higher concentrations

GG.description: Fully functional OR5A1, highly sensitive to violet, floral, and berry aromas (beta-ionone)

description: OR5A1 variant determines ability to perceive beta-ionone, the characteristic scent of violets, berries, and many flowers. One of the best-characterized human olfactory receptor variants.
notes: Beta-ionone is found in roses, violets, raspberries, and many foods. ~50% of people are sensitive to beta-ionone. The variant explains why some people love floral perfumes while others find them imperceptible.

CC.description: Can taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds strongly (supertaster possible)

CG.description: Can taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds (medium sensitivity)

GG.description: Cannot taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds or very weakly

description: TAS2R38 bitter taste receptor variant (PAV/AVI haplotype). Determines ability to taste phenylthiocarbamide (PTC) and PROP bitterness.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: One of three TAS2R38 SNPs forming the PAV/AVI haplotype that determines PTC/PROP bitter taste sensitivity. PAV homozygotes are 'supertasters,' AVI homozygotes are non-tasters. The trait follows near-Mendelian inheritance with ~75% accuracy.

TT.description: Reduced bitter taste sensitivity (part of AVI haplotype)

CT.description: Intermediate bitter taste sensitivity

CC.description: Enhanced bitter taste sensitivity (part of PAV haplotype)

description: Another TAS2R38 variant that combines with rs713598 to form PAV/AVI haplotypes determining bitter taste sensitivity.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: Part of the TAS2R38 haplotype for bitter taste perception. Works in combination with rs713598 and rs10246939. The three SNPs together determine taster/non-taster status with high accuracy across all studied populations.

TT.description: Enhanced bitter taste sensitivity (part of PAV haplotype)

CT.description: Intermediate bitter taste sensitivity

CC.description: Reduced bitter taste sensitivity (part of AVI haplotype)

description: Third key TAS2R38 variant completing the PAV/AVI haplotype. Works with rs713598 and rs1726866.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: Third SNP in the TAS2R38 bitter taste haplotype. The combined PAV/AVI haplotype is one of the best-characterized taste genetics examples. Non-taster frequency varies by population: ~30% in Europeans, ~3% in sub-Saharan Africans.

CC.description: More sensitive TRPV1 receptor, lower capsaicin tolerance, perceives spicy food as more intense

CT.description: Intermediate TRPV1 sensitivity, moderate capsaicin perception

TT.description: Less sensitive TRPV1 receptor, higher capsaicin tolerance, better handles spicy food

description: TRPV1 I585V variant affects the transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 channel, the primary capsaicin (chili pepper) and heat-pain receptor. Determines spice sensitivity and pain threshold.
notes: TRPV1 is activated by capsaicin, heat (>43C), and acidic pH. The I585V variant affects receptor sensitivity and desensitization kinetics. Also influences thermal pain perception and migraine susceptibility.

CC.description: Normal cilantro taste - perceives herb flavor

AA.description: Strong soapy/unpleasant taste perception

description: Genetic variation affecting olfactory receptor determining whether cilantro tastes like herbs or soap.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2012) - A genetic variant near olfactory receptor genes influences cilantro preference

notes: Identified in a 23andMe GWAS as associated with perceiving cilantro as soapy-tasting. Located near olfactory receptor gene OR6A2. Effect is moderate — not all carriers dislike cilantro, and cultural exposure can modify preference. Well-replicated finding.

AA.description: Higher pain sensitivity

AG.description: Average pain tolerance

GG.description: Lower pain sensitivity - higher tolerance

description: COMT haplotype variant modulating catecholamine levels and pain perception thresholds.

sources.name: Diatchenko L et al. (2005) - Genetic basis for individual variations in pain perception and the development of a chronic pain condition

notes: COMT haplotype variant influencing pain sensitivity through altered enzyme activity and mRNA structure. Part of a multi-SNP COMT haplotype — individual effect is modest. Pain perception is highly complex with major environmental and psychological components.

TT.description: Lower celiac/gluten sensitivity risk

TC.phenotype: Carrier for HLA-DQ2 Celiac Risk Allele
TC.description: Carrier for HLA-DQ2 celiac risk allele

CC.description: Higher celiac disease and gluten sensitivity risk

description: HLA-DQ2 haplotype tag SNP strongly associated with celiac disease susceptibility.

sources.name: van Heel DA et al. (2007) - A genome-wide association study for celiac disease identifies risk variants in the region harboring IL2 and IL21

notes: HLA-DQ2.5 haplotype tag SNP — primary genetic risk factor for celiac disease. HLA-DQ2/DQ8 is necessary but not sufficient (~30-40% of the population carries it, but only ~1% develops celiac). Environmental trigger is gluten exposure. Gold standard for celiac genetic risk assessment.

GG.description: Low sensitivity to trigger sounds

GA.description: Moderate sound sensitivity

AA.description: High sensitivity to chewing/repetitive sounds

description: Genetic predisposition to strong emotional reactions triggered by specific sounds like chewing.

sources.name: Sanchez-Roige S et al. (2017) - Genome-wide association studies of impulsive personality traits and misophonia

notes: TENM2 variant linked to misophonia (sound sensitivity) in a single GWAS. Evidence is preliminary — misophonia genetics is poorly understood. The condition likely involves multiple genes and neurological pathways. Replication in larger cohorts is needed.

CC.description: Low motion sickness risk

CT.description: Moderate motion sickness risk

TT.description: High susceptibility to motion sickness

description: Genetic variation affecting vestibular-visual conflict processing and nausea response.

sources.name: Hromatka BS et al. (2015) - Genetic variants associated with motion sickness point to roles for inner ear development, neurological processes and glucose homeostasis

notes: Identified in a 23andMe GWAS for motion sickness susceptibility. Effect is modest. Motion sickness is influenced by vestibular function, visual processing, and psychological factors. Genetic contribution is polygenic.

AA.description: Normal opioid receptor, higher baseline pain sensitivity, better response to opioid medications

AG.description: Intermediate pain sensitivity and opioid response

GG.description: Altered opioid receptor (Asn40Asp), reduced pain sensitivity, may need higher opioid doses

description: OPRM1 mu-opioid receptor variant (A118G) affecting pain perception and opioid medication response. G allele reduces receptor expression.

sources.name: Fillingim RB et al. (2005) - The A118G single nucleotide polymorphism of the mu-opioid receptor gene (OPRM1) is associated with pressure pain sensitivity in humans

notes: OPRM1 A118G variant affecting mu-opioid receptor function. Associated with altered pain sensitivity and variable analgesic response to opioids. Clinical relevance for pain management is being studied but is not yet standard of care. Effect varies by ancestry.

AA.description: Gain-of-function SCN9A variant, lower pain threshold, heightened nociception

AG.description: Heterozygous SCN9A variant, mildly modified pain perception

GG.description: Standard SCN9A function, population-average pain sensitivity

description: SCN9A encodes the Nav1.7 sodium channel, a key pain signaling molecule. Variants cause phenotypes ranging from congenital insensitivity to pain to extreme pain disorders.
notes: SCN9A/Nav1.7 is a validated analgesic drug target. Loss-of-function mutations cause congenital insensitivity to pain, while gain-of-function causes erythromelalgia and paroxysmal extreme pain disorder.

GG.description: Typical pitch perception

GA.description: Enhanced pitch memory

AA.description: Predisposition for absolute pitch ability

description: HLA region variant associated with rare absolute pitch ability in musicians.

sources.name: Theusch E et al. (2009) - Genome-wide study of families with absolute pitch reveals linkage to 8q24.21 and locus heterogeneity

notes: HLA-A region variant associated with absolute pitch in a linkage study. Evidence is preliminary — absolute pitch has a strong genetic component (~80% heritability) but the specific loci remain poorly characterized. Early musical training is a required environmental cofactor.

CC.description: Normal light tolerance

CT.description: Mild light sensitivity

TT.description: Increased light sensitivity (photophobia)

description: NAD(P)H dehydrogenase variant affecting retinal sensitivity to bright light.

sources.name: Siegel D et al. (1999) - NAD(P)H:quinone oxidoreductase 1: role as a superoxide scavenger

notes: NQO1 Pro187Ser variant reducing enzyme activity to near zero. Originally studied for cancer susceptibility. Association with photophobia is indirect and based on limited evidence. NQO1 deficiency affects oxidative stress response in multiple tissues.

GG.description: Susceptible to norovirus

GA.description: Partial resistance

AA.description: Resistant to most norovirus strains (non-secretor)

description: Fucosyltransferase variant determining secretor status and norovirus binding.

sources.name: Thorven M et al. (2005) - A homozygous nonsense mutation (428G→A) in the human secretor (FUT2) gene provides resistance to symptomatic norovirus (GGII) infections

notes: FUT2 nonsense variant conferring near-complete resistance to norovirus infection in homozygotes (~20% of Europeans). Non-secretors (AA genotype) lack the gut surface receptor the virus uses for entry. One of the clearest examples of infection resistance genetics.

GG.description: Standard umami perception

GA.description: Enhanced umami sensitivity

AA.description: Highly sensitive to umami/savory taste

description: Taste receptor variant determining sensitivity to glutamate and savory flavors.

sources.name: Shigemura N et al. (2009) - Genetic and molecular basis of individual differences in human umami taste perception

notes: TAS1R1 variant associated with sensitivity to umami (savory) taste. Less well-characterized than bitter taste genetics. Umami perception is influenced by multiple receptor variants and dietary experience.

CC.description: Standard sweet taste perception

CT.description: Slightly enhanced sweet sensitivity

TT.description: Enhanced sweet taste sensitivity - prefers less sugar

description: Sweet taste receptor variant affecting sensitivity to sugars and sweet taste preference.

sources.name: Fushan AA et al. (2009) - Allelic polymorphism within the TAS1R3 promoter is associated with human taste sensitivity to sucrose

notes: TAS1R2 variant influencing sweet taste perception and sugar intake. Effect is moderate — sweet preference is heavily influenced by environment, age, and dietary habits. Some evidence links this variant to differences in sucrose detection thresholds.

AA.description: Normal TOMM40/APOE region expression, population-average aging trajectory

AG.description: Intermediate effect on mitochondrial import and aging-related biomarkers

GG.description: Altered TOMM40 expression, associated with faster cognitive decline and biological aging markers

description: TOMM40 (translocase of outer mitochondrial membrane 40) variant near APOE affects mitochondrial protein import efficiency. Associated with biological aging rate and cognitive decline.
notes: TOMM40 is adjacent to APOE on 19q13. Debate continues whether TOMM40 has independent effects beyond APOE linkage. TOMM40 poly-T repeat length correlates with age of Alzheimer's onset.

CC.description: Proline variant, stronger cell cycle arrest and DNA repair, slower apoptosis

CG.description: Heterozygous, balanced apoptosis and repair capacity

GG.description: Arginine variant, more efficient apoptosis, potentially better tumor suppression but faster aging

description: TP53 codon 72 polymorphism (Arg72Pro) affects p53 protein function, balancing apoptotic efficiency versus DNA repair capacity. Modifies cancer susceptibility and aging-related phenotypes.
notes: Arg72 has 15x stronger apoptotic activity than Pro72, but Pro72 is better at cell cycle arrest and repair. Population frequencies vary by latitude — Pro72 is more common near the equator.

AA.description: Full XPC function, efficient global genomic nucleotide excision repair

AC.description: Intermediate XPC activity, slightly reduced UV damage recognition

CC.description: Lower XPC efficiency, impaired recognition of UV-induced DNA damage, increased skin aging

description: XPC Ala499Val variant affects the global genome nucleotide excision repair (GG-NER) damage sensor. XPC initiates repair of UV-induced cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers and 6-4 photoproducts.
notes: XPC is the primary UV damage sensor for transcriptionally silent DNA. Homozygous XPC loss causes xeroderma pigmentosum group C. Common variants modestly affect skin cancer risk and photoaging.

AA.description: Increased nevus (mole) count and elevated melanoma susceptibility

AG.description: Moderate effect on mole count and melanoma risk

GG.description: Fewer nevi, baseline melanoma risk from this variant

description: MTAP 9p21 variant affects melanocyte proliferation and nevus formation. Nevus count is the strongest single risk factor for melanoma, and this variant modulates both.
notes: MTAP is adjacent to CDKN2A, the familial melanoma gene. Total nevus count >100 increases melanoma risk ~7x. This variant explains ~1% of nevus count variation but has strong epidemiological relevance.

CC.description: Lower stretch mark tendency

CT.description: Moderate stretch mark risk

TT.description: Higher stretch mark susceptibility

description: Elastin gene variant affecting skin elasticity and striae formation.

sources.name: Tung JY et al. (2013) - A genome-wide association analysis identifies genetic loci associated with stretch marks

notes: ELN region variant associated with stretch mark (striae distensae) susceptibility in GWAS. Stretch marks are primarily influenced by rapid skin stretching (growth, pregnancy, weight change) and skin elasticity. Genetic contribution is one factor among many.

GG.description: Normal MC1R function, typical sun sensitivity for skin type

GA.description: Partial MC1R dysfunction, burns more easily, higher melanoma risk

AA.description: Reduced MC1R function, very sun sensitive, high melanoma risk

description: MC1R variant (V92M) associated with sun sensitivity and melanoma risk, even in individuals without red hair.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (V92M) associated with sun sensitivity and fair skin, though with lower penetrance than classic red-hair MC1R variants. Common across multiple populations. Carriers may have increased UV sensitivity without exhibiting red hair.

GG.description: Normal UV damage recovery

CC.description: Slower sunburn recovery and higher UV sensitivity

description: Melanocortin receptor variant affecting melanin production and UV damage repair.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (D294H) associated with red hair, fair skin, and increased UV sensitivity. Carriers experience slower sunburn recovery and higher melanoma risk. Sun protection is especially important for MC1R variant carriers regardless of apparent hair color.

CC.description: Reduced TERC expression, shorter leukocyte telomere length

CT.description: Moderate TERC activity, average telomere maintenance

TT.description: Higher TERC expression, longer leukocyte telomere length, potentially slower cellular aging

description: TERC encodes the RNA component of telomerase, essential for telomere maintenance. Variants affect leukocyte telomere length, a biomarker of biological aging.
notes: TERC provides the template for telomeric repeat addition. Along with TERT, it is a major genetic determinant of telomere length. Shorter telomeres associate with cardiovascular disease and mortality.

CC.description: Increased TERT-CLPTM1L expression, longer telomeres but higher skin cancer susceptibility

CT.description: Moderate telomere maintenance, intermediate skin cancer risk

TT.description: Lower TERT-CLPTM1L activity, slightly shorter telomeres but reduced skin cancer susceptibility

description: TERT-CLPTM1L 5p15.33 variant influences telomere length and has pleiotropic effects on multiple cancer types, particularly basal cell carcinoma and melanoma.
notes: 5p15.33 locus contains both TERT and CLPTM1L genes. Pleiotropic cancer risk — the same allele can increase risk for some cancers while decreasing risk for others. Major skin cancer susceptibility locus.

AC.description: Heterozygous TERT variant, intermediate telomere maintenance capacity

AA.description: Lower TERT expression, tendency toward shorter telomeres and accelerated cellular aging

CC.description: Higher TERT expression, better telomere maintenance, associated with both longevity and increased cancer risk

description: TERT (telomerase reverse transcriptase) variant affects telomere length maintenance. Longer telomeres are associated with longevity but paradoxically also with increased cancer risk.
notes: TERT is the catalytic subunit of telomerase. The C allele associates with longer telomeres but also increased lung cancer, glioma, and bladder cancer risk — illustrating the aging-cancer tradeoff.

GG.description: Can detect asparagus metabolite odor in urine

GA.description: Moderate detection ability

AA.description: Cannot detect asparagus urine odor

description: Olfactory receptor variant determining ability to smell asparagus metabolites in urine.

sources.name: Pelchat ML et al. (2011) - Excretion and perception of a characteristic odor in urine after asparagus ingestion: a psychophysical and genetic study

notes: Associated with the ability to detect asparagus metabolite odor in urine. Approximately 40% of people cannot smell the characteristic odor. The trait is harmless and primarily of scientific curiosity. Effect is population-consistent.

CC.description: Altered CDKN2A/B expression, faster cellular senescence and biological aging markers

CT.description: Moderate effect on cell cycle regulation and senescence

TT.description: Normal CDKN2A/B expression, population-average biological aging

description: CDKN2A/B locus encodes p16INK4a and p15INK4b tumor suppressors that regulate cellular senescence. Variants affect both type 2 diabetes risk and biological aging rate.
notes: 9p21 is the most replicated GWAS locus, associated with coronary disease, type 2 diabetes, and glioma. p16INK4a is a key senescence marker that accumulates with age.

CC.description: Stronger body odor

CT.description: Moderate body odor

TT.description: Reduced body odor

description: ATP-binding cassette transporter variant affecting apocrine sweat gland secretions.

sources.name: Yoshiura K et al. (2006) - A SNP in the ABCC11 gene is the determinant of human earwax type

notes: Linked to ABCC11 function affecting apocrine gland secretions and body odor intensity. Most relevant in populations polymorphic for ABCC11 variants. The same biological pathway controls earwax type (wet/dry) and axillary odor production.

CC.description: Wet, sticky earwax (European/African type), active apocrine glands, more body odor

CT.description: Wet earwax (dominant), normal body odor

TT.description: Dry, flaky earwax (East Asian type), reduced apocrine gland activity, less body odor

description: ABCC11 variant determining earwax type and apocrine gland secretion. TT genotype (dry earwax) is nearly fixed in East Asians (80-95%) but rare elsewhere.

sources.name: Yoshiura K et al. (2006) - A SNP in the ABCC11 gene is the determinant of human earwax type

notes: Single SNP determining wet vs dry earwax type. The dry earwax allele (T) is near-fixation in East Asian populations. Also determines axillary (underarm) body odor intensity — dry earwax carriers produce less odorous secretions. Classic example of pleiotropic genetic variation.

CC.description: Efficient mitochondrial import of MnSOD, better superoxide radical defense

CT.description: Moderate mitochondrial MnSOD levels, intermediate antioxidant defense

TT.description: Reduced mitochondrial MnSOD import, lower superoxide defense, potential oxidative stress

description: SOD2 Ala16Val variant affects mitochondrial targeting sequence of manganese superoxide dismutase, the primary mitochondrial antioxidant enzyme. Ala16 allows efficient mitochondrial import.
notes: The Ala variant forms an alpha-helix allowing efficient mitochondrial import. Val forms a beta-sheet, reducing import by 30-40%. Paradoxically, higher SOD2 activity has been linked to both protection and risk in different contexts.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; strongly associated with spontaneous clearance of hepatitis C virus infection (~60-80% clearance in CC individuals). The C allele prevents IFNL4 (IFN-lambda-4) protein production, paradoxically improving IFN signaling response. C allele ~72% in Europeans, ~40% in Africans.

CT.description: Heterozygous; intermediate probability of spontaneous HCV clearance. One T allele allows partial IFNL4 expression, partially dampening IFN-alpha/beta signaling efficacy. Also shows intermediate response to peginterferon/ribavirin treatment.

TT.phenotype: Lower HCV Clearance — Chronic Infection Risk
TT.description: Homozygous alternate; strongly associated with failure to clear HCV spontaneously and chronification. The T allele induces IFNL4 protein which paradoxically impairs downstream IFN-alpha/beta signaling. T allele ~60% in Africans, ~28% in Europeans, ~5% in East Asians.

description: IFNL4 encodes interferon lambda-4, a type III interferon discovered in 2013. Paradoxically, the T allele at rs12979860 creates a functional IFNL4 protein that impairs — rather than enhances — the hepatic antiviral response by inducing ISG (interferon-stimulated gene) expression that desensitizes cells to subsequent interferon-alpha/beta signaling. The C allele disrupts IFNL4 open reading frame, preventing this protein and allowing more effective IFN signaling. This explains the long-observed association between this locus and both spontaneous HCV clearance and treatment response to interferon-based therapies.

sources.name: Thomas DL et al. (2009) - Genetic variation in IL28B and spontaneous clearance of hepatitis C virus

notes: This is one of the most clinically validated pharmacogenomic loci in hepatology. The counterintuitive biology (a gene that makes an interferon but hurts interferon responses) makes this a fascinating teaching example in genetics. Strong population stratification: C allele ~72% European, ~40% African, ~95% East Asian — reflecting different evolutionary HCV exposure histories. In the era of direct-acting antivirals (DAAs), this variant no longer affects treatment selection for HCV, but it remains clinically relevant for understanding spontaneous clearance probability and for contexts where interferon therapy is still used. Discuss HCV testing and treatment options with an infectious disease or hepatology specialist.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; functional mannose-binding lectin (MBL) with normal oligomerization and complement lectin pathway activation. Standard innate immune defense against pathogens with mannose/N-acetylglucosamine surface patterns. ~93-96% globally.

CT.phenotype: Reduced MBL Levels — Partial MBL Deficiency
CT.description: Heterozygous; one T allele encodes an MBL2 variant with impaired oligomerization. Circulating MBL levels are approximately 50% of normal. Mild increased susceptibility to recurrent infections in infancy and early childhood.

TT.phenotype: Very Low MBL — Functional MBL Deficiency
TT.description: Homozygous alternate; extremely low or absent functional MBL in serum. T allele ~3.5% globally (~22% in Africans, ~0.09% in East Asians). TT genotype is extremely rare globally but found at higher rates in African populations.

description: MBL2 encodes mannose-binding lectin, an oligomeric plasma protein of the collectin family that recognizes carbohydrate patterns on microbial surfaces and activates the lectin complement pathway (via MASP-1/2). The rs1800451 variant (Gly54Asp, exon 1, codon 54) encodes a structural amino acid change that disrupts MBL collagen-like domain oligomerization, preventing formation of high-molecular-weight multimers required for complement activation. Carriers of the T allele have dramatically reduced serum MBL and impaired complement lectin pathway activity, predisposing to recurrent respiratory, gastrointestinal, and fungal infections particularly in young children and immunocompromised individuals.

sources.name: Garred P et al. (1999) - Susceptibility to HIV and progression of AIDS in relation to variant alleles of mannose-binding lectin

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The T allele is extremely rare in East Asians (~0.09%) but common in Africans (~22%), with Europeans intermediate (~1.8%). The high African T allele frequency may reflect selective pressure from endemic infectious diseases where partial MBL deficiency may provide a heterozygous advantage (similar to sickle-cell heterozygosity). MBL deficiency is clinically relevant for immunocompromised individuals, transplant recipients, and infants with recurrent infections — in these contexts, MBL2 genotyping is sometimes performed. Complement pathway tests (CH50, MBL levels) can quantify functional MBL. Discuss with an immunologist if recurrent unexplained infections are a concern.

TT.description: Less attractive to mosquitoes

CC.description: More attractive to mosquitoes

description: HLA-associated body odor compounds affecting attractiveness to mosquitoes.

sources.name: Fernandez-Grandon GM et al. (2015) - Heritability of attractiveness to mosquitoes

notes: HLA-region variant loosely associated with mosquito bite attraction. Evidence is limited and the mechanism is poorly characterized. Mosquito attraction depends on skin microbiome, CO2 output, body heat, and many non-genetic factors.

CC.description: No photic sneeze reflex

CT.description: Mild sun-induced sneezing

TT.description: Strong sneeze reflex when exposed to bright light

description: Autosomal dominant trait causing sneezing when suddenly exposed to bright light.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Associated with photic sneeze reflex (ACHOO syndrome) in GWAS. The reflex affects ~25% of people and is harmless. Genetic architecture is poorly understood. This SNP explains only a small fraction of the trait.

AA.description: Cannot roll tongue into tube shape

AG.description: Partial tongue rolling ability

GG.description: Can easily roll tongue into tube shape

description: Ability to curl the lateral edges of the tongue upward into a tube shape.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Associated with tongue rolling ability in consumer genetics platforms. The genetics of tongue rolling have been debated for decades — it is NOT a simple Mendelian trait as commonly taught. Monozygotic twin concordance is only ~70%, indicating environmental influence.

AA.description: Increased CYP1A2 induction, very fast caffeine clearance, caffeine has short-lived effects

AC.description: Enhanced CYP1A2 inducibility, moderately fast caffeine metabolism

CC.description: Standard CYP1A2 activity at this position, typical caffeine half-life

description: CYP1A2*1F variant affects inducibility of the primary caffeine-metabolizing enzyme. Ultra-rapid metabolizers clear caffeine faster and may benefit from pre-exercise caffeine for performance.
notes: CYP1A2 metabolizes ~95% of caffeine. This variant modifies enzyme inducibility by smoking, cruciferous vegetables, and other environmental factors. Interacts with rs762551 for overall caffeine metabolism speed.
